section.title: Paul's Joy at Their Repentance
section.sectionRationale: Resuming the appeal broken off at 6:13, Paul reopens his heart and then pours out the relief and joy that Titus's report has brought him. His severe letter had grieved the Corinthians, but their grief proved to be godly sorrow that produced genuine repentance. Here Paul gives the classic distinction between godly sorrow that leads to salvation without regret and worldly sorrow that only brings death, and rejoices that his confidence in them has been fully restored.
section.thesis: Godly sorrow, unlike the worldly grief that ends in death, produces repentance that leads to salvation without regret; and the fruit of such repentance in the Corinthians has turned Paul's anguish into overflowing comfort, joy, and restored confidence.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the reopened heart and Paul's comfort at Titus's arrival; the heart of the passage, godly sorrow versus worldly sorrow and the fruit of true repentance; and Paul's restored confidence and shared joy at Titus's refreshment.

units.label: Comforted by Titus's arrival
units.note: make room for us in your hearts

verses.text: Make room for us in your hearts. We have wronged no one, we have corrupted no one, we have exploited no one.
I do not say this to condemn you. I have said before that you so occupy our hearts that we live and die together with you.
Great is my confidence in you; great is my pride in you; I am filled with encouragement; in all our troubles my joy overflows.
For when we arrived in Macedonia, our bodies had no rest, but we were pressed from every direction; conflicts on the outside, fears within.
But God, who comforts the downcast, comforted us by the arrival of Titus,
and not only by his arrival, but also by the comfort he had received from you. He told us about your longing, your mourning, and your zeal for me, so that I rejoiced all the more.

units.label: Godly sorrow and worldly sorrow
units.note: repentance that leads to salvation without regret

verses.text: Even if I caused you sorrow by my letter, I do not regret it. Although I did regret it, for I see that my letter caused you sorrow, but only for a short time,

yet now I rejoice, not because you were made sorrowful, but because your sorrow led you to {{metanoia|repentance}}. For you felt the sorrow that God had intended, and so were not harmed in any way by us.
Godly {{lype|sorrow}} brings repentance that leads to salvation without regret, but worldly sorrow brings death.

Consider what this godly sorrow has produced in you: what earnestness, what eagerness to clear yourselves, what indignation, what alarm, what longing, what zeal, what vindication! In every way you have proved yourselves to be innocent in this matter.
So even though I wrote to you, it was not on account of the one who did wrong or the one who was harmed, but rather that your earnestness on our behalf would be made clear to you in the sight of God.
On account of this, we are encouraged. In addition to our own encouragement, we were even more delighted by the joy of Titus. For his spirit has been refreshed by all of you.
Indeed, I was not embarrassed by anything I had boasted to him about you. But just as everything we said to you was true, so our boasting to Titus has proved to be true as well.
And his affection for you is even greater when he remembers that you were all obedient as you welcomed him with fear and trembling.
I rejoice that I can have complete confidence in you.

metanoia.gloss: Repentance, literally a change of mind that turns into a change of life. It is more than regret or feeling bad; it is a turning of the whole person away from sin and back to God. Paul rejoices not that the Corinthians were saddened but that their sorrow produced this genuine turning. True metanoia leads to salvation and leaves no regret, because it is the fruit God intended.

lype.gloss: Sorrow, grief, pain of heart. Paul distinguishes two kinds. Godly sorrow is grief felt as God intends, over sin against Him, and it drives one to repentance and life. Worldly sorrow is mere remorse over consequences, wounded pride, or being caught, and it festers into death. The same word covers both; what matters is the direction the sorrow takes the heart.

basic.context: Paul picks up the appeal he left at 6:13, asking the Corinthians to make room for him in their hearts. He had come into Macedonia worn down and anxious, but God comforted him through Titus, who brought good news: Paul's painful letter had grieved the Corinthians, and their grief had turned into real repentance. Paul then explains the difference between godly sorrow, which leads to life, and worldly sorrow, which leads to death, and rejoices that his confidence in them is now complete.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a deeply pastoral passage about how loving correction is meant to work. Paul is relieved not that they were hurt but that their hurt bore the right fruit. The key is verses 10 and 11: read the difference between the two sorrows, and notice the concrete evidences of true repentance Paul lists. Sorrow is not the goal; repentance that turns to God and issues in changed action is.
basic.originalLanguages: The turning point is metanoia, repentance, a change of mind that becomes a change of life, not mere regret. Paul contrasts two kinds of lype, sorrow: godly grief over sin that drives one to God and life, and worldly grief that festers into death. The same pain can run in opposite directions.
basic.typology: The prophets called Israel to return to the Lord with true repentance rather than empty remorse, and Paul's godly sorrow fulfills that call under the new covenant. The comfort God brings to the downcast through Titus reflects the God who comforts His afflicted people throughout Scripture, ultimately in Christ.
basic.christ: The salvation to which godly sorrow leads is salvation in Christ, whose death makes repentance a road to life rather than despair. Because Christ has borne sin, the sorrow of the penitent is not crushing but hopeful, leading to reconciliation. Paul's ministry of correction serves Christ's work of presenting a pure and restored church to Himself.
basic.god: God is the one who comforts the downcast and who intends a sorrow that heals. He stands behind the whole process: the letter, the grief, the repentance, and the joy are all under His sight and for His purpose. He is the God who turns even painful discipline toward the salvation and good of His people.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 51 is the model prayer of godly sorrow and repentance. Matthew 27 shows the worldly remorse of Judas that ended in death. Hebrews 12 speaks of the painful discipline that yields the peaceful fruit of righteousness.
basic.oneStory: God has always called His people not merely to feel bad over sin but to turn back to Him and live. Here that turning bears fruit through Paul's hard letter, and joy replaces grief. It is the pattern of the whole gospel: sorrow over sin, met by the God who comforts, leading through repentance to salvation in Christ.

context.literary: This section resumes the personal appeal interrupted at 6:13 by the call to separation, picking up with make room for us in your hearts. It completes the long travel narrative begun in 2:12 to 13, where Paul left off anxiously awaiting Titus in Macedonia. The passage closes the first major movement of the letter on a note of restored joy before Paul turns to the collection in chapters 8 and 9.
context.historical: After a painful visit and a severe, tearful letter, Paul had sent Titus to Corinth and then waited in agonized suspense for news. The Corinthians had a wrongdoer to discipline and a strained relationship with Paul to mend. Titus's report that they had responded with grief, repentance, and renewed loyalty is what turns Paul's anxiety into the overflowing relief of these verses.
context.canonical: The distinction between godly and worldly sorrow became a foundational text for the Christian understanding of true repentance. Its portrait of correction that heals rather than harms informs the church's practice of loving discipline, and its list of the fruits of repentance in verse 11 has long guided pastoral discernment of genuine change.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To reassure the Corinthians of his love, to explain and vindicate the painful letter he sent, and to rejoice that their godly sorrow bore the fruit of true repentance, thereby restoring the mutual confidence and affection between apostle and church.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative of Titus, the letter, and Paul's relief describes a particular history. The teaching of verses 10 and 11 is prescriptive and enduring: godly sorrow leads to repentance and salvation, worldly sorrow leads to death, and genuine repentance shows itself in concrete fruit.

debates.question: What was the severe or tearful letter Paul refers to?

views.label: A lost letter written between 1 and 2 Corinthians
views.case: Many hold that Paul wrote a now lost, painful letter after the sorrowful visit, distinct from 1 Corinthians, since the emotional distress described does not match 1 Corinthians well.

views.label: First Corinthians itself
views.case: Others identify the severe letter with 1 Corinthians, which contains sharp rebuke, taking Paul's language of causing sorrow to refer to that canonical letter.

debates.bearing: The doctrine of the passage stands either way; whichever letter is meant, the point is that loving, painful correction produced godly sorrow and true repentance rather than harm.

debates.question: What is the difference between godly sorrow and worldly sorrow?

views.label: A difference of object and direction
views.case: Godly sorrow grieves over sin against God and turns the heart to Him in repentance and life; worldly sorrow grieves over consequences, shame, or being caught and turns inward to despair and death.

views.label: A difference chiefly of outcome
views.case: Some stress the fruit as the test: the same feeling of grief is godly if it ends in repentance and salvation, worldly if it ends in death, so the outcome reveals which it was.

debates.bearing: The two readings reinforce each other: sorrow rightly directed toward God bears the fruit of repentance and life, and the presence of that fruit, listed in verse 11, is how godly sorrow is known.

study.originalLanguages: The hinge of the passage is metanoia, repentance, which is not a passing feeling of regret but a change of mind that turns the whole life back to God. Paul carefully distinguishes two forms of lype, sorrow. Godly lype is grief felt as God intends, grief over sin against Him, and it drives the heart toward repentance, salvation, and life, leaving no regret behind. Worldly lype is remorse over consequences, wounded pride, or exposure, and turned in on itself it festers into death. The same word, the same ache of heart, runs in opposite directions depending on whether it turns the sinner toward God or away from Him; and the genuineness of the Corinthians' repentance is proved by its fruit, the earnestness, longing, and zeal Paul catalogues.

typology.type: The prophetic call to return to the Lord
typology.fulfillment: Godly sorrow that turns to God rather than empty remorse fulfills the prophets' summons to true repentance, now realized under the new covenant in Christ.
The comfort God brings Paul through Titus reflects the God of all comfort who comforts His afflicted people, supremely in the consolation of Christ.

study.christ: The salvation to which godly sorrow leads is salvation secured in Christ; because He bore sin at the cross, the penitent's grief becomes a road to life rather than a pit of despair. Repentance is safe and hopeful only because there is a Savior to turn to. Paul's costly work of correction serves Christ's purpose to cleanse and restore His church, and the joy of reconciliation between apostle and people is a small picture of the reconciliation Christ works between God and sinners.
study.god: God is the God who comforts the downcast, and He stands behind every part of this story: He intended the sorrow, He worked the repentance, and He brought the joy. He is the God who disciplines those He loves for their good, turning even painful correction toward their salvation. His hand is seen in the timely arrival of Titus and in the changed hearts of the Corinthians, so that the whole episode magnifies His comforting, restoring grace.

crossReferences.note: David's prayer of godly sorrow and repentance, the broken and contrite heart God will not despise.
Judas's worldly remorse that led to death, the opposite of the sorrow that leads to salvation.
The painful discipline of the Lord that yields the peaceful fruit of righteousness for those trained by it.
God as the Father of compassion and God of all comfort, who comforts us in all our troubles, seen here in the comfort through Titus.

study.oneStory: Throughout Scripture God calls His people not merely to feel remorse over sin but to turn back to Him and live. In this passage that turning bears fruit through Paul's hard letter, and grief gives way to joy. It is the gospel in miniature: sorrow over sin, met by the God who comforts the downcast, leading through true repentance to salvation in Christ and restored fellowship.

quiz.q: Why did Paul rejoice over the sorrow his letter had caused?

options.text: Because their sorrow led them to genuine repentance, not because they were hurt.
options.note: Paul rejoices in the fruit of repentance, not in their pain itself.

options.text: Because he enjoyed having power to make them grieve.
options.note: Paul explicitly says he does not rejoice that they were made sorrowful.

options.text: Because their sorrow proved they feared him.
options.note: The point is repentance toward God, not fear of Paul.

options.text: Because it settled an old personal grudge.
options.note: Paul acts for their good and God's glory, not personal grievance.

quiz.q: What is the difference between godly sorrow and worldly sorrow?

options.text: Godly sorrow produces repentance leading to salvation; worldly sorrow brings death.
Godly sorrow is more intense; worldly sorrow is milder.
options.note: The difference is direction and fruit, not intensity.

options.text: Godly sorrow is only for pastors; worldly sorrow is for everyone else.
options.note: Both kinds of sorrow are possible for anyone; the issue is where the sorrow leads.

options.text: There is no real difference between them.
options.note: Paul draws a sharp and vital distinction between the two.

quiz.q: What evidence shows the Corinthians' repentance was genuine?

options.text: It produced earnestness, longing, indignation, alarm, zeal, and eagerness to make things right.
options.note: Verse 11 lists the concrete fruits of true repentance.

options.text: They simply felt bad for a while and moved on.
options.note: Feeling bad is not repentance; genuine repentance bears fruit.

options.text: They paid Paul a large sum of money.
options.note: The evidence is changed hearts and action, not payment.

options.text: They wrote an angry reply defending themselves.
options.note: True repentance showed as eagerness to clear themselves before God, not self justification.

quiz.q: How did God comfort the anxious Paul in Macedonia?

options.text: Through the arrival of Titus and his report of the Corinthians' repentance.
options.note: God, who comforts the downcast, comforted Paul by Titus's good news.

options.text: By removing all conflict from his life at once.
options.note: Paul still faced conflicts outside and fears within; comfort came through Titus.

options.text: By a private vision unrelated to the church.
options.note: The comfort came through Titus and the Corinthians' response, not a vision.

options.text: By telling him to abandon the Corinthians.
options.note: God restored the relationship rather than ending it.

questions: Paul distinguishes godly sorrow that leads to life from worldly sorrow that ends in death. When you grieve over sin, does it drive you to God or only into shame and self pity?
True repentance showed itself in concrete fruit, not just feelings. What would earnestness, longing, and zeal to make things right look like in an area you need to repent of?
Paul's painful letter was an act of love, not cruelty. How do you respond to loving correction, and what might God be teaching you through it?
God comforted the downcast Paul through Titus and through the church's response. Who has God used to comfort you, and whom might He want to comfort through you?
Paul's confidence and joy in the Corinthians were fully restored after repentance. Is there a broken relationship where repentance could open the way to renewed trust?

translationNotes.note: The dash of the source is removed; rendered we were pressed from every direction; conflicts on the outside, fears within, keeping Paul's stark description of his distress.
The dashes of the source are removed and the verse rendered as its own sentence ending in a comma; the thought continues into verse 9, which now begins yet now I rejoice.

Godly sorrow renders lype felt as God intends; the same word covers worldly sorrow, so the difference lies in whether the grief turns the heart to God in repentance, metanoia, or in on itself toward death.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Corinthians 7:2-16. Em dashes removed per house style: 7:5 rendered with a semicolon (we were pressed from every direction; conflicts on the outside, fears within), and 7:8 rendered without dashes, ending in a comma and continuing into verse 9 (yet now I rejoice). Sermon set to none; not verified this session.
